1: Joshua summons the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh — the Transjordan tribes who had agreed to help with the conquest before receiving their territory east of the Jordan (Num 32; Joshua 1:12-18). The formal summoning signals a formal occasion: this is the dismissal ceremony, the completion of the obligation. The narrative takes the word-keeping of these tribes as the model of covenant fidelity to be honored and commended before the whole assembly.
2: atem shemartem et kol asher tzivah Moshe eved YHWH etchem: ‘you have kept all that Moses the servant of YHWH commanded you’ — Joshua’s opening commendation is total: et kol, all that was commanded. This is the highest possible evaluation — complete covenant fidelity. The Transjordan tribes, who might have been tempted to return to their own territory once it was secured, chose instead to fulfill their commitment through the years of the western conquest. Their loyalty is now formally recognized before the whole congregation.
3: lo azavtem et acheichem zeh yamim rabim ad hayom hazeh: ‘you have not deserted your brothers these many days, right up to this day’ — the time qualification (“these many days”) acknowledges the sacrifice involved: years away from home, wives, children, and livestock in their own territory, while serving in the western campaign. Faithfulness that endures over time, not just in the initial enthusiasm of commitment, is the quality Joshua commends. The same quality governs the NT call to perseverance: “let us not grow weary of doing good” (Galatians 6:9).
4: veattah heniyach YHWH Eloheichem le'acheichem ka'asher diber lahem: ‘now the LORD your God has given rest to your brothers as he promised them’ — the dismissal is grounded in divine fulfillment: the rest has been given. The Transjordan tribes can go home not because they chose to leave early but because the purpose of their service is complete. Joshua’s release of them is simultaneous with YHWH’s fulfillment of the rest-promise. Timing the release to the fulfillment of the purpose is itself an act of covenant honor.
5: rak shimru me'od la'asot et hamitzvah ve'et hatorah asher tzivah Moshe eved YHWH le'ahavah et YHWH Eloheichem ulelechet bekol derachav velishmor mitzvotav udovqah vo ule'ovdo bechol levavchem uvechol nafshchem: ‘only be very careful to observe the commandment and the law… to love YHWH your God, walk in all his ways, keep his commandments, cling to him, and serve him with all your heart and soul’ — the parting charge is the fullest statement of covenant obligation in Joshua, a near-verbatim anticipation of the Shema’s full form. The five verbs (love, walk, keep, cling, serve) cover the full range of covenantal response. Dabaq (cling) is the same verb used in Genesis 2:24 (“a man shall leave his father and mother and cling to his wife”) — covenant faithfulness is as intimate as marriage.
6: vayevarechem Yehoshua vayishlachem vayelechu el ohaleihem: ‘Joshua blessed them and sent them on their way, and they went to their tents’ — the blessing and dismissal completes the formal ceremony. The Transjordan tribes depart with Joshua’s blessing — the same blessing that Moses gave in Deut 33 before his death. The narrative places Joshua in the Moses-role: the one who blesses the departing people at the completion of the redemptive act.
7: The narrative notes a particular complexity: Moses had given the eastern half of Manasseh land in Bashan, but Joshua had given the western half of Manasseh land in Canaan proper. The same tribe straddles both sides of the Jordan — a geographic expression of the mixed situation of the Transjordan arrangement. The blessing was for both halves, the charge was the same, but the territories are separated by the river that Israel crossed as a covenant act.
8: shuvú el ohaleihem becharmishah rabah: ‘return to your homes with great wealth’ — the dismissal includes material provision: livestock, silver, gold, bronze, iron, clothing — the plunder of the western campaign divided with the Transjordanites. The instruction to divide the plunder with your brothers is a cross-reference to the principle of Numbers 31:27 (split the plunder between the soldiers who went to battle and the rest of the congregation). Those who serve on the front lines share their gain with the community; those who remain share with those who went.
9: The Transjordan tribes depart from Shiloh — the tabernacle’s location in the west — and cross back into Gilead, their own territory. The phrase “the land of their possession, which they received according to the commandment of YHWH through Moses” emphasizes the legitimacy of their eastern territory: it was YHWH’s assignment through Moses, not a second-best compromise. The narrative is careful to not treat the Transjordan settlement as a failure or an inferior arrangement.
10: vayavou el gelilot haYarden asher be'eretz Kena'an vayivnu bnei Reuven uvnei Gad vachazi shevet haMenashe sham mizbeach al haYarden: ‘they came to the region of the Jordan in the land of Canaan and built an altar there by the Jordan’ — the altar is built on the Canaanite side of the Jordan (“the land of Canaan”) before crossing over — deliberately visible to the western tribes. The location matters: it is not hidden in Gilead but positioned as a visible marker at the border. The intent (as revealed in v24-28) is to be seen by both sides.
11: The report reaches the Israelites: the Transjordan tribes have built an altar “at the frontier of the land of Canaan in the region about the Jordan, on the side belonging to the people of Israel” — the geography of the altar’s location is already potentially inflammatory. An altar on the Canaanite side of the Jordan could mean an attempt to establish a rival sacrificial site, directly violating the centralization principle of Deut 12 (you shall not offer your burnt offerings in every place that you see).
12: vayishme'u bnei Yisrael vayiqahel kol adat bnei Yisrael Shiloh la'alot aleihem latzava: ‘the entire assembly of the Israelites gathered at Shiloh to go up against them in war’ — the immediate response of the western tribes is military: the altar constitutes a potential covenant breach serious enough to justify civil war. The assembly at Shiloh — the location of the tabernacle — grounds the military threat in covenantal authority: the community gathers at the place of YHWH’s presence to prosecute YHWH’s cause. The speed of the response demonstrates how seriously the covenant centralization of worship was taken.
13: Before going to war, the Israelites send a delegation — Phinehas and ten leaders. The substitution of diplomacy for immediate war is itself a covenantal act: the law required attempted reconciliation before conflict. Phinehas is the ideal delegate: he is the priest who demonstrated covenantal zeal at Peor (Numbers 25:7-8) and whose covenant of peace (Numbers 25:12-13) made him YHWH’s faithful instrument against covenant violation. He represents both the prosecution of covenant faithfulness and the possibility of restoration.
14: The ten leaders — one from each tribe of Israel — represent the covenantal whole in the delegation. The number ten for the tribal representation alongside Phinehas reflects the structure of Numbers 13-14, where tribal representatives went ahead. The delegation is an act of solidarity: the covenant community acts together, through its representative leaders, rather than through mob action or unilateral military decision.
15: The delegation arrives in Gilead and addresses the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-Manasseh. The dramatic confrontation is between Israelites — not between Israel and Canaanites. The danger of the situation is precisely that the covenant community is fracturing from within. The altar at the Jordan, whatever its intent, has created a crisis of communal solidarity that is more threatening than external enemies.
16: mah hama'al hazeh asher me'altem be'Elohei Yisrael: ‘what is this breach of faith (ma'al) that you have committed against the God of Israel?’ — ma'al is the same term used of Achan’s theft (Joshua 7:1) and of unauthorized use of sacred things in Leviticus 5. The accusation is specific and serious: this is not mere civil conflict but a trespass against the sacred. The delegation presents the strongest possible interpretation of the altar’s significance — a charitable impulse requires giving the accused the chance to explain, not presuming guilt — while naming what would be true if the worst interpretation were correct.
17: hameut lanu et avon Peor: ‘is the sin of Peor too small for us?’ — the reference to the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25) invokes the most recent devastating covenant failure, which killed 24,000 people and which Phinehas himself had stopped with his spear. The delegation’s appeal to Peor’s memory is simultaneously a warning (that’s what covenant violation produces) and a confession: “we have not fully cleansed ourselves of it.” The community’s moral accountability for past covenant failures is presented as an ongoing weight.
18: veattim tashuvu hayom me'acharei YHWH vehayah atem tomredu hayom baYHWH umachar el kol adat Yisrael yiqtzof: ‘and if you rebel against the LORD today, tomorrow he will be angry at the whole assembly of Israel’ — the delegation articulates the principle of corporate solidarity in covenant: one tribe’s sin affects the whole assembly. This is not arbitrary collective punishment but the nature of covenant membership: the community is one body, and infection in one part spreads to the whole (cf. 1 Corinthians 5:6: “a little leaven leavens the whole lump”). The concern for the whole assembly’s welfare drives the confrontation.
19: ve'im teme'ah artzechem ivru lachem el eretz achuzat YHWH asher shaken bo mishkan YHWH: ‘if your land is unclean, come over into the land of the LORD’s possession where the LORD’s tabernacle dwells’ — the remarkable offer: if the Transjordan territory cannot support proper worship, the western tribes will make room for you among us. The offer reveals that the delegation’s primary concern is covenant fidelity, not territorial defensiveness. The principle that access to YHWH’s tabernacle matters more than tribal territory is a covenant priority that the NT confirms: “do not give up meeting together” (Hebrews 10:25) — proximity to the assembly of God’s people is worth significant sacrifice.
20: halo Achan ben Zerah ma'al ma'al bachareim veal kol adat Yisrael hayah qatzef: ‘did not Achan son of Zerah commit a trespass regarding the devoted things, and wrath came upon the whole congregation of Israel?’ — the Achan precedent is invoked precisely: one man’s ma'al brought YHWH’s anger upon the whole assembly, and “he was not the only one who died for his iniquity.” The delegation is not threatening the Transjordan tribes but warning them about the corporate consequences of what YHWH’s judgment looks like when covenant breach goes unaddressed.
21: vaya'anu bnei Reuven uvnei Gad vachazi shebet haMenashe: the Transjordan tribes answer the delegation — their response will be decisive for the outcome. The three-tribe unity in the response mirrors the three-tribe unity of the altar’s construction; they act as one in explanation as they did in building. The answer begins with the solemn divine invocation of v22 before the explanation of v24.
22: el Elohim YHWH el Elohim YHWH hu yodea veYisrael hu yeda: ‘The Mighty One, God, the LORD! The Mighty One, God, the LORD! He knows, and let Israel know!’ — the triple divine name invocation (el, elohim, YHWH) is the most solemn oath formula in the text. The repetition of the triple name twice creates a six-element invocation of divine witness. The declaration “he knows” (hu yodea) appeals to divine omniscience as the ultimate court of appeal: if they have sinned, God knows; if they have not sinned, God knows. The accusation that requires YHWH as witness is the highest possible formal denial.
23: The denial is categorical: if the altar was for burnt offerings or grain offerings or sacrifices — if it was an attempt to establish an independent sacrificial site — then “may the LORD himself call us to account.” The conditional self-imprecation (calling YHWH’s judgment on themselves if they are guilty) is the formal structure of oath-taking in ancient Near Eastern legal contexts. By swearing this oath, they place themselves under divine judgment for lying — the highest possible guarantee of truthfulness.
24: ume'da'agah midavar patzinu et zot: ‘but we did this out of anxiety (midavar da'agah), for a specific reason’ — the anxiety is about future exclusion: “your children may in time say to our children, ‘What have you to do with the LORD, the God of Israel?’” The concern is intergenerational covenant exclusion — the fear that the Jordan river will become a theological boundary as well as a geographic one. The future-generation concern reveals covenantal thinking: they are building for their descendants’ covenant security, not for their own present advantage.
25: gevul sam YHWH beinenu uveneichem bnei Reuven uvnei Gad et haYarden ein lachem cheleq baYHWH: ‘the LORD has made the Jordan a border between us and you — you have no portion in the LORD’ — this is the feared future exclusion, put in the mouths of the western tribes’ children. The Jordan as theological boundary rather than merely geographic boundary would mean that the Transjordan tribes’ covenant status depends on their geographic location rather than their YHWH-allegiance. The altar is a physical counter-argument to this feared interpretation.
26: vayomru nivneh lanu et mizbeach lo le'olah velo lezevach: ‘we said, let us build an altar — but not for burnt offerings or sacrifices’ — the explicit denial that the altar is for sacrifice distinguishes it from a rival sanctuary. The altar as witness rather than altar as worship-site is a category that Deuteronomy 12 does not address: the centralization law prohibits sacrificial worship outside the chosen place, not commemorative or testimonial monuments. The Transjordan tribes are drawing a legal distinction between a sacrificial altar and a witness-altar.
27: ki ed hu beinenu uveneichem uvein doreinu acharenu la'avod et avodat YHWH lefanav: ‘it is a witness between us and you and between our generations after us, that we do perform the service of the LORD’ — the altar as ed (witness/testimony) is a category familiar from ancient Near Eastern treaty monuments: masseboth (standing stones) and boundary markers served as covenant witnesses (cf. Genesis 31:44-52, Laban and Jacob’s witness-heap). The altar’s function is not sacrificial but testimonial — a physical declaration of covenant participation available for future generations to point to.
28: veyomru eleynu ue'el doreinu machar veamarnu re'u et tavnit mizbeach YHWH: ‘and in the future when they say to us or to our descendants: look, the copy of the altar of YHWH’ — the witness-altar is designed as an argument for intergenerational covenant inclusion. If future children ask “what right do you have?”, the altar is the answer: our fathers built this as a copy of the altar of YHWH; we have the same relationship to the God of Israel as the western tribes. The altar is a piece of tangible evidence that can be pointed to in a dispute about covenant standing.
29: chalilah lanu mimreod laYHWH ulesubvav hayom me'acharei YHWH livnot mizbeach le'olah laminchah velazevach milevad mizbeach YHWH Eloheinu asher lifnei mishkano: ‘far be it from us to rebel against the LORD and turn away from him today, to build an altar for burnt offerings, grain offerings, or sacrifices, other than the altar of the LORD our God that stands before his tabernacle!’ — the conclusion is the categorical denial combined with the affirmation: there is one altar, the one before the tabernacle; we have not built another. The chalilah lanu (far be it from us) is the strongest possible Hebrew expression of rejection and horror at the suggested possibility.
30: Phinehas and the leaders heard the Transjordan tribes’ explanation and were pleased. The narrative satisfaction of the delegation’s acceptance is the resolution of the tension built since v12. The theological issue (potential covenant breach) is resolved by the Transjordan tribes’ explanation; the community is not divided. Phinehas — who stopped the Peor apostasy with violent action — here is the instrument of reconciliation through hearing and discernment. Both actions serve covenant fidelity; the choice between them depends on what the situation actually requires.
31: hayom yadanu ki betochenu YHWH asher lo me'altem baYHWH hama'al hazeh az hitzaltem et bnei Yisrael miyad YHWH: ‘today we know that the LORD is among us, because you have not committed this breach of faith against the LORD — now you have rescued the people of Israel from the hand of the LORD’ — Phinehas’s declaration reads the Transjordan tribes’ faithfulness as evidence of divine presence among the whole people. Their non-sin is not merely a private relief but a communal rescue: the whole assembly was in danger of divine wrath for a ma'al that turned out not to have occurred. Faithfulness in a potential crisis situation rescues more than the faithful — it protects the whole community.
32: Phinehas and the leaders return from Gilead to Canaan and bring back a good report to the Israelites. The narrative structure is complete: crisis (v10-12), investigation (v13-29), resolution (v30-31), report (v32-33). The structure mirrors diplomatic and legal processes that are recognizable across cultures: confrontation, inquiry, adjudication, reconciliation. The covenant community resolves its internal crisis through process, not through preemptive violence.
33: vayitav hadavar be'einei bnei Yisrael vayevarachu Elohim bnei Yisrael: ‘the report pleased the Israelites, and they praised God’ — the community’s response to the resolution is worship, not merely relief. The conflict that threatened civil war is resolved by the Transjordan tribes’ fidelity, and the proper response is praise to YHWH. The connection between covenant resolution and worship is consistent: “Therefore, if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you… first go and be reconciled to your brother, then come and offer your gift” (Matthew 5:23-24).
34: vayiqre'u livnei Reuven uvnei Gad et hamizbeach ki ed hu veinenu: ‘the Reubenites and Gadites named the altar Ed — “Witness” — saying, “It is a witness between us that the LORD is God’” — the naming closes the narrative with the altar’s function permanently inscribed in its name. The altar named Ed (witness) stands as a permanent testimony to covenant unity across the Jordan — not a rival worship site but a monument to the shared covenant that transcends the river boundary. Every future generation that sees the altar knows: the Transjordan tribes are part of the covenant people of YHWH.
